question_title: Will the United States and Iran hold direct, in-person talks between July 11 and July 23, 2026?
summary: Resolving YES requires two things within a shrinking 10-day window: de-escalation enough for talks to resume, AND those talks being in the DIRECT face-to-face format Iran has consistently refused. Before the July escalation, direct Doha talks were actually expected in the third week of July, which would have made this near coin-flip. But the July 7-13 escalation (ceasefire over, 300+ strikes, Iran attacking the mediators themselves on July 12, closing Hormuz, refusing to negotiate under attack) has derailed the track and hardened Iran's posture. Only a mediated phone call — not an in-person meeting — was reported near-term. Direct-talk precedent in this series and the fact that ceasefire negotiations qualify keep the probability off the floor, but active conflict with Iran attacking mediators makes a same-room bilateral session unlikely. Final estimate ~13%.

factors.description: Status quo: US-Iran technical track has been INDIRECT all of 2026; Iran explicitly refuses direct same-room talks ('no plans for negotiations with the American side at any level').
Pre-escalation, officials EXPECTED high-level DIRECT talks in Doha in the 'third week of July' (~Jul 13-19), inside the window (Anadolu Jul 2, Dawn Jul 5).

Major escalation Jul 7-13 derailed the track: ceasefire declared over, 300+ US strikes, Iran attacked 5 Gulf states including mediators Qatar/Oman on Jul 12, closed Hormuz, refused to negotiate under attack. Dominant recent negative.

Precedent for direct rounds exists in this series (Islamabad Apr: 2/3 rounds direct; Lucerne Jun: some direct talks); fine print counts ceasefire negotiations, enabling a possible high-level direct circuit-breaker under 60-day MoU deadline pressure.

10 days remain; wars can flip to sudden ceasefire+diplomacy, but a shift to the DIRECT format Iran consistently refuses, during active bombing, is a high bar. Requires conjunction of de-escalation AND direct format.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 13% (NO likely)**

The question asks whether the US and Iran will hold direct, face-to-face talks between July 11–23, 2026.

**Key reasoning:**
- **Status quo strongly favors NO**: The entire 2026 negotiation track has been indirect (via Oman/Qatar/Pakistan), and Iran has explicitly and repeatedly refused same-room direct talks.
- **A real upward pull existed**: Pre-escalation, sources reported officials *expected* direct high-level talks in Doha during the third week of July — squarely inside the window.
- **But the escalation reversed it**: The July 7–13 military escalation (ceasefire declared over, 300+ US strikes, Iran attacking five Gulf states *including the mediators* Qatar and Oman on July 12, closing Hormuz, and refusing to negotiate under attack) derailed the diplomatic track. The only near-term contact reported was a mediated phone call, not an in-person meeting.
- **Not negligible**: Direct rounds have precedent in this series (Islamabad April, Lucerne June), ceasefire negotiations count under the fine print, and 10 days remain in a volatile situation where wars can flip to sudden diplomacy.

The conjunction required — de-escalation *and* a direct format Iran keeps refusing, during active bombing — makes YES unlikely but not impossible.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, at any point from July 11, 2026 through July 23, 2026 (inclusive, all dates local to the venue), officials representing the government of the United States and officials representing the government of Iran meet face-to-face in the same room for a negotiating session — that is, direct (not solely indirect/mediated) talks — as reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). Talks conducted only through an intermediary (e.g., Oman, Qatar, or Pakistan carrying messages between delegations sitting in separate rooms) do NOT count. It resolves NO if no such direct, in-person meeting is reported to have occurred in that window.
fine_print: A "negotiating session" means an interaction focused on the U.S.-Iran bilateral agenda (nuclear program, sanctions, frozen assets, regional security, or a ceasefire), not an incidental encounter (e.g., passing each other at a multilateral summit, a group photo, or a courtesy handshake at an unrelated event). At least one participant on each side must be a serving government official or an officially designated envoy of that government. A meeting counts if credible reporting states the U.S. and Iranian delegations were together in the same room for at least part of the session, even if mediators were also present. The meeting must physically occur within the window; an agreement or announcement merely to hold future direct talks does not by itself count.

factors.description: Throughout 2026 the US-Iran technical track has been INDIRECT via mediators; Iran explicitly and repeatedly refuses direct same-room talks ('no plans for negotiations with the American side at any level'). Status quo strongly favors NO.

Before the escalation, multiple sources (Anadolu Jul 2, Dawn Jul 5) reported officials EXPECTED high-level DIRECT negotiations in Doha in the 'third week of July' (~Jul 13-19), directly inside the window. Strong upward pull if track had held.

Major military escalation Jul 7-13 derailed the diplomatic track: ceasefire declared over, 300+ US strikes, Iran attacked 5 Gulf states INCLUDING mediators Qatar and Oman on Jul 12, closed Hormuz, said it won't negotiate while under attack, 'era of one-sided deals is over.' Dominant recent factor, strongly negative.

Precedent for direct rounds exists in this very negotiation series (Islamabad Apr: 2 of 3 rounds direct; Lucerne Jun: 'some direct talks'), and fine print counts ceasefire negotiations. A high-level direct circuit-breaker meeting is conceivable given 60-day MoU deadline pressure.
10 days remain in window; wars can flip to sudden ceasefire+diplomacy, but a shift to the specific DIRECT face-to-face format Iran has consistently refused, during active bombing, is a high bar.

input.anchor: Base rate for a specific format-shift (indirect to direct) event in a 2-week window given a party's standing refusal: low, ~15-20%. Adjusted by the specific near-term expectation vs escalation.
input.assessment: The question hinges on two things happening within a shrinking 10-day window: (1) de-escalation enough for talks to resume, and (2) those talks being in the DIRECT face-to-face format Iran has consistently refused. Before the July escalation, direct talks in Doha in the third week of July were actually expected, which would have made this ~coin-flip-favorable. But the July 7-13 escalation (ceasefire declared over, 300+ strikes, Iran attacking the mediators themselves on July 12, closing Hormuz, refusing to negotiate under attack) has derailed the track. Iran is hardening, not softening. The only near-term contact mechanism reported is a mediated phone call, not an in-person meeting. Direct-talk precedent exists in this series and ceasefire negotiations count under the fine print, so a high-level direct circuit-breaker is not negligible — but during active conflict with Iran attacking mediators, a same-room bilateral session is unlikely. I land around 12%.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether a sudden ceasefire materializes and whether resumed talks would be direct or revert to indirect. Iran's July 12 posture is the key negative signal.
input.tool_audit: research() sub-agent returned rich, well-sourced findings directly on point (format status, escalation timeline, prior expectations for third week of July). No failures. Sufficient to forecast without further tools given the question closes today.
input.update_triggers: Announcement of a ceasefire + face-to-face Witkoff/Araghchi meeting; or continued strikes with no diplomatic contact.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was excellent here — it surfaced both the pre-escalation expectation of direct Doha talks AND the July 12 escalation that reverses it, which is exactly the tension needed. For a fast-breaking geopolitical question closing same-day, single research call was efficient. No friction.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I verified all five factors against the research trace and the fresh worldview research entry (us-iran-nuclear-negotiations-july-2026-direct-face-e9eb232b, state=fresh), and found no fabrication, double-counting, wrong-direction, or misalignment errors.

HALLUCINATION CHECK — all clear:
- Factor 1 (indirect track, Iran's standing refusal, "no plans for negotiations at any level"): grounded in key_facts and trace lines 31-33.
- Factor 2 (Anadolu Jul 2, Dawn/Independent Pakistan Jul 5, expected DIRECT talks in Doha "third week of July"): grounded verbatim in trace and worldview sources (aa.com.tr, independent-pakistan.com).
- Factor 3 (300+ strikes over three nights, Iran attacked 5 Gulf states incl. mediators Qatar/Oman Jul 12, Hormuz closed, "era of one-sided deals is over"): fully grounded. The quote is correctly attributed to chief negotiator Qalibaf (July 12, Straits Times). Escalation timeline matches ISW report.
- Factor 4 (Islamabad Apr: 2 of 3 rounds direct; Lucerne Jun: "some direct talks"; 60-day MoU): grounded in Wikipedia sources and data_points.

SIGN CHECK — all correct. Positive factors (2: +1.3 pre-escalation expectation; 4: +0.7 precedent) pull toward YES as described; negative factors (1, 3, 5) pull toward NO as described.

RESOLUTION CRITERIA — provided and well-engaged. The agent correctly applies the "same room / direct not mediated" distinction, notes the mediated phone call does not qualify, and correctly invokes the fine print that ceasefire negotiations count.

PRE-PUBLICATION EVENT — no problem. Window opens July 11, published July 13. No factor claims a qualifying direct meeting already occurred in the July 11-13 pre-publication window; the only reported contacts were a failed July 11 Muscat Iran-Oman meeting and a mediated multi-party phone call, neither of which resolves YES. The forecast is forward-looking, not built on a premature-resolution claim.

PROBABILITY — my independent estimate lands ~12-15%. A standing Iranian refusal to same-room talks, combined with active bombing, Iran attacking the very mediators, and explicit "won't negotiate under attack" statements, strongly favors NO. This is partially offset by genuine pre-escalation momentum toward a direct Doha round in this exact window and real precedent for direct rounds in this series (plus ceasefire-negotiation qualification). The agent's 12% is well within my range.

COUNTERARGUMENT — strong and non-token: it constructs the coherent YES story (pre-escalation direct-Doha expectation, refusal-as-posture, war-driven ceasefire contact, 60-day deadline pressure) and quantifies an alternative 25-35% band with a concrete falsification condition.

SELF-ASSESSMENT — 0.62 confidence is appropriately calibrated for a volatile 10-day active-war scenario.

TOOL AUDIT — agent's "no failures" claim matches ground truth (0 errors / 23 calls).

No errors found beyond trivial narrative rounding. Forecast is evidence-driven and consistent with the worldview store.
